column14: In the Spring of 2019, we decided to expand upon our Little Free Pantry in the courtyard of House of Prayer Lutheran Church by adding a refrigerator to the porch that could be accessed by anyone, 24 hours per day - the same as our Little Free Pantry. Dr. Ken Cirka purchased a commercial grade double door refrigerator and donated it to us to use in helping the community access perishable food items.  For over a year now, due to his generosity and the generosity of donors of items, we have been able to provide our community in need with items ranging from milk, eggs, cheese, fresh produce, ready-to-eat foods and many other items that require refrigeration!
Community fridge located in Fort Greene, Brooklyn. Accessible 24/7 for anyone to take what they need and share what they can.

Open Source Gallery has installed a mini-fridge outside the gallery for use by the community. Neighbors who are able are encouraged to bring labelled food in containers or prepackaged food for the fridge. Anyone is welcome to take food from the fridge!

Currently, the gallery is being used as an Upcycle Shop by local teens. All are welcome to bring bicycles or household items in need of repair, as well as ideas for diy projects, and teens at the shop will do their best to repair and/or complete them for no charge!

Forest Ave Come Unity fridge is a free community fridge for Staten Island’s North Shore community. This community faces extreme levels of food insecurity, food dessert, food waste and health disparities compared to the rest of Staten Island. Therefore, this mutual aid initiative works to provide fresh, healthy food that is otherwise inaccessible. The food is from the community, for the community and stands on the values of interdependence, community restoration and community empowerment.

CFF is on the land of the Stó:lō Nation. We are a community free fridge with hopes of bridging class gaps and improving access to fresh foods for all of our community members. CFF also operates a small zine library with writing and art from queer, trans, Indigenous, BPOC, disabled, and other marginalized writers and artists. The library is often stocked with abolitionist and revolutionary material, as well as science-fiction, fantasy, and speculative writing. 
CFF has a 40" wide ramp from the driveway to the porch of 8870 Edward Street for accessibility.

The most northly freedge (Frísskápur in Icelandic) today (07.2021) has been realized to serve the Icelandic capital Reykjavík. It is hosted at Andrými the radical social centre in Reykjavík. Kamila and Marco had long been working on ideas to reduce food waste and finally joined the Freedge community. Coloured as the colors of the bees it inspires to bee free from waste and save delicious food that our loved pollinators kindly make grow.
Let's save some food together, donate and share in the heart of Reykjavík!

Farm Collective (no fridge).

PFC supports marginalized and underserved farmers in Western New York needing access to clean, rural farmland, farming and business education, and the opportunity to farm for income. PFC is the only regional, non-profit farm program offering education and opportunity for long-term, secure land access to BIPOC and new American farmers. Communities join PFC to build fresh food security within their communities; grow culturally relevant crops; connect to agricultural heritage, and reap the physical and mental health benefits the farm provides. 

The farm also donates and sells its harvest to its local communities.

column14: As part of our commitment to our community, and via a partnership with our friends at New Purpose Thrift Store, we have created the "Community (Free)dge" initiative. This initiative specifically focuses on providing free food to individuals and families in need of food items. The fridge is meant to help individuals and families cover their immediate food needs and is not meant to fill personal fridges on a monthly basis. We partner with numerous other non-profit organizations and local businesses in order to maximize the effort and reach of this program.

column14: Opened in April 2022, the Fredericksburg Free Fridge & Pantry serves to fight food insecurity, reduce food waste, and support the greater Fredericksburg community by sourcing food donations from individuals and organizations. The Fredericksburg Free Fridge & Pantry aims to address the issues of food waste and food insecurity by tackling them both at the same time, collecting surplus food to prevent further waste and providing it free of charge to those experiencing food insecurity. The fridge is outfitted with safety locks, wireless thermometers that notify our team's cell phones if the temperatures in the fridge start to warm, and a camera for extra safety. Visitors can now access a guest Wifi hotspot in the Upper Garden. Our volunteer Free Fridge Heroes monitor the fridge and help the project run smoothly, cleanly, and safely. A volunteer will be by daily to ensure that the fridge and pantries are clean, and that all available food is safe for consumption.

column14:
Roots For Life, in collaboration with Common Good City Farms, proudly announces the launch of the F.R.E.S.H. Community Fridge initiative. This program aims to address food insecurity and promote community well-being by providing accessible, nutritious food to those in need.

F.R.E.S.H. (Feeding Residents Every Single Hour) Community Fridge will serve as a local resource where community members can both take and leave food items, fostering a sense of sharing and support within the neighborhood. By working together, we are committed to reducing food waste and ensuring that everyone has access to healthy options.

We invite community members to participate by donating fresh produce, pantry staples, and ready-to-eat meals to the fridge. Moreover, we encourage local residents to utilize this resource as needed. This initiative exemplifies our dedication to cultivating a sustainable, equitable food system and enhancing the overall health of our community.

Together, we can create a resource that nourishes and uplifts our neighbors, strengthening our ties and promoting a culture of kindness and collaboration.

column14:
The Gwen Hale Resource Center was opened in 2018 by Dr. Gwen Hale, Director of the Writing Center at UMW. She was one of the driving forces behind the formerly named Eagle Resource Closet. The Center partners with the Fredericksburg Area Food Bank, Giant Foods, UMW Dining Services and many other on- and off-campus entities for donations. ​Our goal is to provide food, clothing, school supplies, toiletries and other items at no cost to any member of the UMW community. Our hope is to eliminate food insecurity. The GHRC is open to all students and members of the community.

The GHRC is located on the 5th floor of Lee Hall. Take the elevator closest to Monroe Hall to floor “A” (for attic). Follow the signs!
